heuristics.rule: Run your CRS score before you choose a program, not after you graduate
heuristics.condition: When a prospective international student is deciding which Canadian program to enroll in
heuristics.action: Use the federal CRS calculator to project your post-graduation score based on age, language, education, and planned work experience before committing to a program
heuristics.because: Most CRS-relevant variables (program type, age at graduation, education level) are locked by enrollment decisions made years before you enter the immigration funnel — late awareness cannot reverse them

heuristics.rule: If your program is a diploma or certificate and your study permit application was filed on or after June 25, 2025, verify PGWP eligibility against the current eligible fields list before enrolling
heuristics.condition: When a diploma or certificate student is selecting a Canadian institution and program
heuristics.action: Cross-check the specific program field against IRCC's post-June 25, 2025 eligible fields list — do not assume eligibility based on older advice or peer experience
heuristics.because: 178 fields were removed from PGWP eligibility; students in ineligible programs will not receive a PGWP regardless of academic performance, leaving them with no legal work authorization after graduation

heuristics.rule: Prioritize French language training if your CRS score is stuck below the general draw cut-off (~520+)
heuristics.condition: When a non-French-speaking Express Entry candidate cannot reach competitive CRS scores through other means
heuristics.action: Invest 6–12 months in French language training to reach CLB 7, unlocking both significant CRS point additions and access to French-language draws with cut-offs as low as 379–428
heuristics.because: French proficiency is the single highest-ROI CRS lever available — it adds points and opens entirely separate draw categories with dramatically lower thresholds

heuristics.rule: Ensure your Canadian work experience is in NOC TEER 0, 1, 2, or 3 — not TEER 4 or 5 — before counting it toward CEC eligibility
heuristics.condition: When a PGWP holder is planning work experience to qualify for Canadian Experience Class
heuristics.action: Look up the NOC TEER classification of any job offer before accepting it, and prioritize roles in TEER 0–3 even if TEER 4–5 roles pay comparably
heuristics.because: CEC requires 1 year of skilled work in TEER 0–3; retail, food service, and manual labor roles (TEER 4–5) do not count, and students who work 1–2 years in ineligible roles must restart the clock

heuristics.rule: Have your spouse take IELTS and document their education credentials as soon as you begin your PGWP period
heuristics.condition: When a married or common-law PGWP holder is building toward an Express Entry application
heuristics.action: Get your spouse's language tested and education assessed (ECA if foreign credentials) — do not wait until you are ready to file
heuristics.because: Spousal language and education scores can add 10–40 CRS points, a meaningful difference at current cut-offs, and assessments take months to complete

heuristics.rule: Monitor PNP draw streams for your NOC code actively — do not wait passively for a federal Express Entry draw
heuristics.condition: When a candidate has a base CRS score below the current federal draw cut-off but has Canadian work experience in a specific occupation
heuristics.action: Identify which provinces have active PNP streams aligned to your NOC code and apply when your score meets provincial thresholds
heuristics.because: A provincial nomination adds 600 CRS points, effectively guaranteeing an ITA regardless of base score — it is often faster than waiting for a general federal draw

heuristics.rule: Apply for PGWP immediately after graduation — do not wait for employer confirmation or job offers
heuristics.condition: When a student completes their eligible Canadian study program
heuristics.action: Submit the PGWP application as soon as your final transcript or graduation confirmation is available, including the now-required language test results
heuristics.because: PGWP applications must be filed while your study permit is still valid or within the implied status period; delays can result in loss of eligibility and gaps in work authorization

heuristics.rule: Do not rely on job offer points as a CRS strategy — they were removed as of March 25, 2025
heuristics.condition: When a candidate is evaluating whether to pursue a specific employer in hopes of boosting their Express Entry score
heuristics.action: Redirect effort previously spent on employer-based CRS boosts toward language scores, French proficiency, and PNP streams
heuristics.because: Job offer points no longer exist in CRS scoring; acting on outdated advice wastes strategic energy that could be spent on still-valid levers

decision_factors.factor: Program type: university degree vs. diploma/certificate
decision_factors.why_it_matters: University degree students (bachelor's and above) retain PGWP eligibility regardless of field; diploma/certificate students are subject to the post-June 2025 eligible fields list, making program selection a direct immigration variable
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm the credential type with the institution (degree vs. diploma/certificate) and check the current IRCC eligible fields list for diploma programs

decision_factors.factor: Study permit application date relative to June 25, 2025
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines which PGWP eligibility rules apply — students who applied before this date are protected under previous rules; those who applied after must meet the new program field requirements
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the date on your study permit application confirmation from IRCC; this is a binary threshold, not a spectrum

decision_factors.factor: French language proficiency (current and achievable level)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: French at CLB 7+ unlocks both additional CRS points and access to French-language draws with cut-offs 90–150 points lower than general draws — this single factor can be the difference between qualifying and not qualifying
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Take a French placement test and estimate realistic time to CLB 7 given current level; compare the CRS gain against other available levers

decision_factors.factor: Age at time of Express Entry application
decision_factors.why_it_matters: CRS age points peak at 20–29 and decrease progressively after 30, accelerating after 35 — a 2-year delay in filing can cost 15–30+ CRS points that cannot be recovered
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Use the CRS calculator to model your score at current age vs. age at graduation vs. 1 year post-graduation; identify whether urgency is warranted

decision_factors.factor: NOC TEER classification of available employment
decision_factors.why_it_matters: CEC requires 1 year of Canadian work experience in TEER 0–3; time spent in TEER 4–5 roles does not count, and students who discover this late must work additional time in eligible roles before applying
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look up the NOC code of any job offer using the ESDC Job Bank NOC search tool and confirm its TEER level before accepting

decision_factors.why_it_matters: For married or common-law candidates, a spouse's CLB 5+ language score and education credentials add meaningful CRS points (estimated 10–40 depending on levels) — potentially closing the gap to an ITA
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Have your spouse take IELTS/TEF and run the CRS calculator with and without spousal factors to quantify the actual point difference

decision_factors.factor: Active PNP streams for your occupation and province of residence
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A provincial nomination adds 600 CRS points, making PNP availability a faster pathway than federal Express Entry for candidates with sub-520 base scores
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Monitor the immigration portal of provinces where you live or work; check NOC-specific draw histories to assess realistic access

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Foreign education credentials must be assessed by a designated ECA organization to receive CRS education points; unassessed credentials receive no points and cannot be appealed retroactively after filing
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Initiate ECA assessment through WES, ICAS, or other IRCC-designated bodies as early as possible — processing takes 3–6 months

decision_factors.factor: Program duration (relative to 2-year threshold for maximum PGWP)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Programs under 8 months generate no PGWP; programs 8 months to under 2 years generate a PGWP equal to program length; programs 2+ years generate the maximum 3-year PGWP — duration directly determines how much time you have to accumulate CEC-eligible work experience
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate the expected PGWP duration from your program length and model whether it provides enough time (1 year minimum) to qualify for CEC before expiry

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Enrolling in a shorter, cheaper college diploma or certificate program to save money
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the program field is not on the post-June 2025 eligible list and the study permit was filed after June 25, 2025, there is no PGWP — the cost savings on tuition are offset by the loss of legal work authorization and the entire immigration pathway
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Cost-conscious international students choosing college over university, especially those who applied for study permits after June 2025

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking any available job during the PGWP period to start earning
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the job is in NOC TEER 4 or 5 (retail, food service, manual labor), it builds no CEC eligibility — the student spends 1–2 years working without advancing toward PR, while also aging out of peak CRS age points
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in fields with limited TEER 0–3 entry-level opportunities, or those who need income quickly and accept whatever is available

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Dismissing French language investment as unnecessary for non-Francophone students
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The student remains locked into general Express Entry draws with 520+ CRS cut-offs while French-language draw cut-offs sit 90–150 points lower — the decision to skip French may mean waiting years longer for an ITA or never qualifying
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mandarin, Hindi, Korean, and other non-European language speakers who view French as irrelevant to their background

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until the PGWP period to start thinking about immigration strategy
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Program type, program field, study permit application date, age trajectory, and education credential type are all already fixed — the most impactful variables can no longer be changed, leaving the student only marginal levers to work with
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who treat immigration planning as a post-graduation task rather than a pre-enrollment one

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on a job offer as a CRS booster in Express Entry strategy
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: As of March 25, 2025, job offer points were removed from the CRS scoring system — students who structured their job search around this strategy gained no CRS benefit from it
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who received pre-2025 immigration advice or relied on outdated online resources

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not pursuing your spouse's language testing and ECA because you plan to apply individually
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Spousal factors in Express Entry add 10–40 CRS points — by not documenting them, the candidate forgoes points that could close the gap to an ITA, often for the cost of one IELTS exam
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Married or common-law PGWP holders who are unaware that spousal factors affect their own CRS score even under a principal applicant filing

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Selecting a program based on career interest alone without checking PGWP or NOC TEER implications
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The student may complete a program that generates either no PGWP or a short-duration PGWP, and then work in a TEER 4–5 role — making the entire Canadian study investment a dead end for permanent residency
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in non-traditional or emerging fields (some agriculture, transport, arts) where PGWP eligibility or TEER classification is ambiguous

failure_patterns.pattern: Enrolling in a diploma program without verifying PGWP field eligibility under the new (post-June 2025) rules
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rely on peer advice, recruitment agents, or institution marketing that predates the June 2025 rule changes — and institutions may not proactively disclose eligibility risk
failure_patterns.consequence: No PGWP issued after graduation; student has no legal work authorization in Canada and must leave or find an alternative permit, effectively losing the investment in Canadian education as an immigration pathway
warning_signs: Choosing a program based on a friend's PGWP experience from before June 2025
Institution or agent says the program 'has always been PGWP eligible' without referencing the current IRCC list
Program is in transportation, certain agriculture fields, or traditional trades

failure_patterns.pattern: Accumulating 1–2 years of TEER 4–5 work experience and then discovering it doesn't count for CEC
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are unaware of NOC TEER categories and assume any legal Canadian work experience counts toward CEC eligibility; they prioritize income or convenience over immigration strategy
failure_patterns.consequence: The student must find a TEER 0–3 job and work another full year before becoming CEC-eligible, often while their PGWP approaches expiry and age penalty increases
warning_signs: Working in retail, food service, warehousing, or manual labor on PGWP
Never having looked up the NOC code of your current job
Assuming 'Canadian work experience' means any work done in Canada

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring CRS score tracking until after graduation, then discovering disqualifying gaps
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat immigration as a post-graduation problem and assume the system will work out if they just get the degree; they experience optimism bias about meeting cut-offs
failure_patterns.consequence: Discovers late that their score is 30–50 points below the cut-off with no remaining high-impact levers — French takes time, age penalty has already begun, and the PGWP clock is running
warning_signs: Has never run the official CRS calculator
Believes Express Entry 'is just about getting a job in Canada first'
Doesn't know their own CLB score or language band results

failure_patterns.pattern: Dismissing French language investment and then being locked out of lower-cut-off draws
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Non-Francophone students perceive French as culturally irrelevant to their background or career, underestimate the CRS point value, and focus exclusively on English proficiency
failure_patterns.consequence: Competes only in general draws at 520+ CRS cut-off rather than French-language draws at 379–428; may wait years longer for an ITA or age out of peak scoring before receiving one
warning_signs: Has never researched French-language Express Entry draw cut-off history
Believes French is only useful for living in Quebec
Actively avoids French language coursework as 'not relevant to my degree'

failure_patterns.pattern: Using pre-2025 immigration advice and expecting job offer points to boost CRS
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rely on immigration blog posts, YouTube videos, or advisor guidance that was accurate before March 25, 2025 but is now outdated; the rule change is recent and not yet widely internalized
failure_patterns.consequence: Job search strategy optimized for CRS point gain yields no CRS benefit; student may have accepted a less suitable role believing it would accelerate PR
warning_signs: Advisor or resource mentions 'getting a job offer to boost your CRS' without noting the 2025 rule change
CRS strategy sheet lists job offer as a key lever
Candidate has never verified when their immigration information source was last updated

failure_patterns.pattern: Not submitting spouse's language and education credentials for ECA/IELTS before filing Express Entry
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students don't realize spousal factors affect the principal applicant's CRS score; they assume only their own credentials matter and don't want to spend money on tests for a non-working spouse
failure_patterns.consequence: Files with a CRS score 10–40 points lower than achievable; may wait additional draw cycles or miss the cut-off entirely when a small boost would have secured an ITA
warning_signs: Married or common-law partner has never taken a Canadian-recognized language test
Foreign credentials of spouse have never been ECA-assessed
Candidate runs CRS calculator without entering spousal information

diagnostic_questions.question: When did you submit your Canadian study permit application — before or after June 25, 2025?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines which set of PGWP eligibility rules applies; students before this date are protected under previous rules, those after must meet new field-of-study requirements
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If before June 25, 2025: your program field does not affect PGWP eligibility — focus on duration and language requirements instead
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If after June 25, 2025: immediately verify your specific program field against the current IRCC eligible fields list — do not assume eligibility

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your Canadian program a university degree (bachelor's or above) or a college diploma/certificate?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: University degree students have blanket PGWP eligibility regardless of field; diploma/certificate students face field-specific eligibility restrictions and higher compliance risk
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If university degree: PGWP field risk is resolved — shift focus to program duration, language test requirements, and CRS planning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If diploma/certificate: immediately check program field against IRCC's eligible list and confirm study permit application date relative to June 25, 2025

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your current CRS score, and have you run the official federal calculator in the last 3 months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Reveals whether the candidate has situational awareness of their immigration competitiveness and which specific gaps exist (language, age, education, spousal factors)
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If score known: compare it against current draw cut-offs for general, CEC-specific, French-language, and PNP-enhanced draws — identify which category is realistic
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If score unknown: run the calculator immediately; no strategic immigration decisions should be made without a current score

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your French language proficiency level, and have you taken an official TEF Canada or TCF Canada test?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines access to French-language Express Entry draws (cut-offs 90–150 points lower than general draws) and whether French investment is a viable CRS lever
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If CLB 7+ French: ensure it is documented in your Express Entry profile and model CRS score with French-language draw eligibility factored in
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If below CLB 7 or untested: estimate realistic time to CLB 7 and calculate the CRS point gain; compare to other available improvement strategies

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the NOC TEER level of the job you are currently in or planning to accept on your PGWP?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether current work experience will count toward the 1-year CEC requirement; TEER 4–5 roles do not qualify and may result in the candidate building time with no CEC eligibility progress
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If TEER 0–3: confirm the NOC code is accurate and start tracking your 1-year eligibility clock — ensure you maintain consistent hours and document employment
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If TEER 4–5: urgently evaluate whether a parallel or alternative TEER 0–3 opportunity is available; every month in an ineligible role delays PR

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your province of residence or work have an active PNP stream aligned to your NOC code?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Identifies whether the 600-point provincial nomination pathway is accessible, which would effectively guarantee an ITA regardless of base CRS score
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If active PNP stream exists: determine the minimum provincial score requirement and apply as soon as eligible — do not wait for a federal draw
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no active stream: monitor draw history of neighboring provinces and assess whether relocating is strategically worthwhile given the 600-point benefit

diagnostic_questions.question: If you have a spouse or common-law partner, have they taken an IELTS/TEF and had their education credentials ECA-assessed?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Identifies uncaptured CRS points from spousal factors — a commonly overlooked source of 10–40 additional CRS points that requires only documentation effort, not changing any other circumstance
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If tested and assessed: include spousal factors in CRS profile and verify score is calculated correctly
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If not tested: initiate IELTS/TEF registration and ECA process immediately — both take months and cannot be rushed when you are ready to file

diagnostic_questions.question: If you completed your highest degree outside Canada, has it been assessed by an IRCC-designated ECA organization?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Unassessed foreign credentials receive no CRS education points; this is a correctable gap but requires 3–6 months to process and cannot be backdated after filing
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If ECA complete: confirm it aligns with the Canadian credential level you are claiming in your Express Entry profile
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If ECA not started: begin immediately through WES, ICAS, or another designated body — do not wait until you are otherwise ready to file

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your current age, and how old will you be at your projected Express Entry application date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: CRS age points peak at 20–29 and decline after 30, accelerating after 35 — a 2-year delay in application timing can cost 15–30+ irrecoverable CRS points, changing whether an ITA is achievable
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If under 29 at application: age is not yet a constraint — focus on other levers; model whether rushing the timeline sacrifices other score components
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If 30 or older, or approaching 30: treat application timing as urgent; each additional year of delay has a compounding negative effect on CRS score

industry_realities.common_belief: If you study in Canada, you will automatically get a PGWP after graduation
industry_realities.actual_reality: PGWP eligibility depends on program type, program field, program duration, study permit application date, and now language test results — multiple students in ineligible programs discover post-graduation that no PGWP will be issued
industry_realities.implication: PGWP eligibility must be verified before enrollment, not assumed; the burden is on the student, not the institution, to confirm they qualify

industry_realities.common_belief: Any Canadian work experience on a PGWP counts toward permanent residency through Express Entry
industry_realities.actual_reality: Only Canadian work experience in NOC TEER 0, 1, 2, or 3 counts toward CEC eligibility; TEER 4–5 work (which includes most entry-level retail, food service, and manual labor roles) generates zero CEC eligibility regardless of duration
industry_realities.implication: Job selection during the PGWP period is an immigration decision, not just a career one — prioritizing TEER 0–3 roles over higher-paying TEER 4–5 roles is often the correct strategic choice

industry_realities.common_belief: Getting a Canadian job offer boosts your Express Entry score significantly
industry_realities.actual_reality: Job offer points were removed from the CRS scoring system on March 25, 2025 — a job offer provides no CRS point boost and does not meaningfully accelerate Express Entry under current rules
industry_realities.implication: Students should stop optimizing their job search for employer-based CRS gains and redirect that strategic energy toward language scores, French proficiency, and PNP streams

industry_realities.common_belief: French is only relevant if you want to live in Quebec or work in a French environment
industry_realities.actual_reality: French language proficiency at CLB 7+ adds significant CRS points and unlocks access to French-language Express Entry draws with cut-offs 90–150 points lower than general draws — it functions as a pure immigration point mechanism independent of where you live or work
industry_realities.implication: Non-Francophone students should evaluate French investment as an immigration optimization tool, not a lifestyle choice; the ROI in CRS points is often higher than any other available lever

industry_realities.common_belief: The Canada immigration system is straightforward because it's points-based and not a lottery
industry_realities.actual_reality: While there is no lottery, CRS cut-off scores have risen significantly (general draws now 520+, CEC-specific 531–534), making competition intense; the system rewards those who optimize across multiple dimensions simultaneously, not just those who show up with Canadian experience
industry_realities.implication: Students who plan only one variable (e.g., 'just get Canadian work experience') and ignore others (French, spousal factors, age timing, PNP) will find themselves below cut-off despite meeting basic eligibility requirements

industry_realities.common_belief: You can start planning your Canadian permanent residency strategy after you graduate
industry_realities.actual_reality: The most impactful CRS variables — program type, program field, age at application, study permit application date — are determined by decisions made before or at enrollment, often 2–4 years before graduation
industry_realities.implication: Immigration strategy must begin at program selection, not at graduation; the PGWP period is too late to recover from enrollment decisions that created structural CRS disadvantages

industry_realities.common_belief: Only your own qualifications matter for Express Entry
industry_realities.actual_reality: For married or common-law candidates, a spouse's language test results and education assessment can add 10–40 CRS points under the spousal factor component — making spouse documentation a direct immigration priority
industry_realities.implication: Married students should treat their spouse's IELTS/TEF exam and ECA as part of their own immigration preparation, not as optional paperwork for the spouse

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes CRS planning begins after graduation when they are on PGWP and looking for jobs — views it as a post-study administrative task
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Runs the CRS calculator before choosing a program and uses projected post-graduation scores to inform program type, duration, and field selection decisions
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The highest-leverage CRS variables are locked at enrollment; a student who discovers their structural disadvantage during PGWP has almost no high-impact remaining options

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Chooses program based on tuition cost, career interest, and school ranking — treats PGWP eligibility as a given
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Filters programs first by PGWP eligibility (checking the current IRCC list for diploma/certificate programs), then by NOC TEER category of likely graduate employment, then by cost and career factors
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A program that fails PGWP eligibility is an immigration dead end regardless of academic quality; a program that leads only to TEER 4–5 employment makes CEC qualification extremely difficult

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Takes the best-paying or most convenient job available, regardless of NOC TEER classification — assumes all Canadian work experience is equivalent for immigration
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Prioritizes TEER 0–3 roles even at marginally lower pay, because NOC classification directly determines CEC eligibility and every month in a TEER 4–5 role is an immigration delay
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A student who spends 1–2 years in TEER 4–5 work must then restart the 1-year CEC eligibility clock while their PGWP expires and their age penalty grows

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Dismisses French as culturally irrelevant and focuses exclusively on advancing English proficiency — never quantifies the CRS impact of French
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Calculates the CRS point gain from reaching CLB 7 in French, compares it to other available levers, and makes a data-driven decision about whether 6–12 months of French study is the highest-ROI immigration action available
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: French-language draws operate at cut-offs 90–150 points below general draws; ignoring this creates a multi-year wait that informed candidates have already eliminated

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of PNP as a pathway
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Views Express Entry as the only federal pathway and waits passively for a general draw — unaware of PNP streams or treats them as complicated and optional
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Actively monitors PNP draw streams for their NOC code and province, treating a provincial nomination as a priority strategy because it adds 600 CRS points and often provides a faster route than federal draws
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A student who qualifies for a PNP and doesn't pursue it may wait years for a federal ITA while their PGWP expires — the 600-point nomination is often the single fastest available path to PR

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Use of spousal factors in Express Entry
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Completes the Express Entry profile using only their own credentials, unaware that spousal factors affect the principal applicant's CRS score
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Has spouse take IELTS/TEF and complete ECA assessment before filing, captures all available spousal CRS points, and models score with and without spousal factors to quantify the difference
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: 10–40 uncaptured CRS points from spousal factors could be the difference between qualifying in a draw and waiting additional cycles — for the cost of one language test

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Knowledge of current CRS rules vs. outdated advice
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Relies on immigration forums, peer advice, or blog posts without checking the publication date — acts on rules that may have changed (e.g., job offer CRS points, PGWP field eligibility)
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Cross-references all strategic decisions against the current IRCC official guidance and checks for rule change dates; treats advisor or peer advice as a starting point for verification, not a final answer
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The March 2025 removal of job offer CRS points and the June 2025 PGWP field eligibility overhaul are recent enough that most peer advice and online resources are still outdated — acting on stale information has direct immigration consequences
